title: Wood selection for pyrography
subtitle: Which species burn well, which to avoid, and why grain matters
excerpt: A guide to choosing wood for pyrography: species with tight even grain that hold fine detail, species with strong grain contrast that fight the burn, and how surface preparation affects the result. Covers sycamore, lime, beech, birch, and the common pitfalls of resinous and coarse-grained species.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for wood properties relevant to pyrography. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on poker-work and material selection.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of burning designs into wood using a heated metal tip.
Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable, low moisture content. Pyrography on damp wood burns unevenly; only seasoned timber should be used.

content.text: is grain density. Fine, even grain burns uniformly and holds crisp line detail. Coarse or alternating grain burns at different rates: the soft early wood darkens quickly and the dense late wood resists, producing a striped effect across every line. In general, species with pale, close-grained timber are the most rewarding.

Sycamore and lime are the two best British species for pyrography. Both have very tight, diffuse-porous grain with almost no growth-ring contrast; a line burns at the same depth across the full width of the ring. Birch is a close second. Beech is acceptable on quartersawn faces where the ray figure is visible but the ring contrast is reduced. All four species should be fully
and free of surface oil before use.

Oak and ash have strong ring contrast. Fine detail burned across the grain will show the ring pattern rather than the intended design. Pine and other softwoods contain resin pockets that spit and smoke at burning temperatures, and the ring contrast is extreme. Walnut is too dark in its natural colour for most pyrography work; the burn does not read clearly against the background.

Burn on the face of the board (the flat-sawn or quarter-sawn surface), not on end grain. End grain burns unpredictably and the tip skips across the exposed fibres. Even on good face grain, knots and local grain disturbance cause harder spots; work around them or accept them as part of the character of the piece.

Sand the surface to 400 grit before burning. A coarser surface burns inconsistently because the tip skips over raised fibres. Do not apply finish before burning; oil fills the surface cells and prevents the tip from making clean contact. After burning, wipe off any carbon residue before applying finish.

Wood that is still partially damp from recent drying burns less crisply and produces more smoke than fully seasoned timber. A spoon or bowl that has been carved from green wood and dried for two weeks is often still above the ideal moisture level for pyrography. Wait until the piece has been dry for at least four weeks and feels uniformly dry to the touch before burning.
